our_argument_summary: Polaris is 0.74° from true pole; the claimed +0.27° excess at 35.9°N falls within the combined error budget (Polaris offset ±0.74°, atmospheric refraction ~0.02°, measurement uncertainty ±0.2–0.5°). No error budget, methodology, or replication is provided. PRED-NEW-001 registered 2026-03-12 after extensive site development — retrodiction not prediction. Dome's own site marks the broader Polaris prediction as FALSIFIED (2026-03-15). Dome's aetheric refraction formula n(r) predicts only ~0.04° at Oslo, not the claimed +4.5°.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitoring exists for WIN-065. PRED-NEW-001 is listed as PENDING in the predictions registry. No automated validation of Polaris measurements appears in the dome's automation code.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Not applicable — no validation exists at all. The +0.27° figure appears only in the coordinate table as a discrepancy between Polaris elevation (36.18°) and WGS84 latitude (35.91°) for Chapel Hill, NC.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Aetheric refraction serves as an unfalsifiable escape clause — any discrepancy between Polaris observation and dome geometry is attributed to refraction with adjustable parameters.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No reference to WIN-065 or PRED-NEW-001 in monitor.py or pull_data.py. No domain, no API fetch, no comparison logic. PRED-NEW-001 remains PENDING in predictions.txt.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard astronomy: Polaris altitude = observer geographic latitude (to arcsecond precision after pole-star corrections). The dome claims the same data pattern (Polaris altitude tracks latitude) but attributes it to dome height function H(r) instead of a spherical Earth. The dome's coordinate table itself demonstrates this: for 23/24 cities, Polaris elevation ≈ WGS84 latitude within ±0.05°.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: PRED-NEW-001 registered 2026-03-12 after V12/V13 coordinate system development. Polaris declination (~89.26°) and altitude-latitude relationships published in FK5, Hipparcos, Tycho-2 catalogs for decades. The dome's own coordinate table uses WGS84 latitudes as Polaris elevation inputs, confirming the data was available before the 'prediction.'
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome's H(r) function does not independently predict what Polaris altitude should be at any location. The coordinate system works in reverse: it takes Polaris elevation as INPUT and solves r × tan(polaris) = H(r) for radial distance r. The +0.27° excess is not a dome prediction — it's a data point in an input table.

holes_found.description: Duplicate paragraph in detail_evidence. The PRED-NEW-001 retrodiction argument appears twice with slightly different wording: 'The dome's PRED-NEW-001, registered 2026-03-12, appeared after extensive site development work and uses Polaris observations published in standard star catalogs for decades — this is retrodiction of known data, not prospective prediction.' is followed by a near-identical paragraph starting with 'The dome's PRED-NEW-001, registered 2026-03-12, appeared after extensive site development work including V13 coordinate system revisions.' Both paragraphs make the same point. One should be removed.
holes_found.recommendation: Delete the second (longer) duplicate paragraph. Keep the first, more concise version. The repetition weakens what is otherwise a strong argument by looking like an editing error.

holes_found.description: Missing killer argument: dome's own coordinate table contradicts PRED-NEW-001. Quantitative analysis shows 23 of 24 cities in the dome's coordinate table have Polaris elevation matching WGS84 latitude to within ±0.05° (London: -0.007°, Paris: +0.043°, Berlin: -0.020°, Oslo: -0.014°, Moscow: -0.006°, etc.). Only Chapel Hill shows a +0.27° excess. PRED-NEW-001 predicts Oslo should show +4.5° excess, but the dome's own coordinate table lists Oslo Polaris at 59.90° (vs WGS84 59.91°), which is essentially 0° excess. The dome's own data directly contradicts its own prediction.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence: 'The dome's own coordinate table lists Oslo Polaris elevation at 59.90° (WGS84: 59.91°N), showing essentially zero excess — directly contradicting PRED-NEW-001's prediction of +4.5° for Oslo. Of 24 cities in the table, only Chapel Hill shows any notable deviation; the other 23 match WGS84 latitude to ±0.05°.'

holes_found.description: The word 'systematic' in the claim and detail_claim is misleading even as a characterization of the dome's position. A single data point (Chapel Hill +0.27° in a table of 24 cities where 23 show ≈0° excess) is the opposite of systematic. Our text should make this clearer — currently the error budget argument handles it, but explicitly noting the one-out-of-24 anomaly is more devastating.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence: 'The 'excess' is not systematic: of 24 cities in the dome's coordinate table, only Chapel Hill shows a notable offset (+0.27°). The other 23 cities match WGS84 latitude to within ±0.05°, consistent with the globe model prediction that Polaris altitude equals geographic latitude.'

holes_found.description: The refraction analysis could be sharper. Our text says the dome's n(r) formula 'would predict ~0.04° of refraction at Oslo.' My quantitative computation of n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(r/20015)² at r=2753 km (Oslo) gives n ≈ 1.00378, which translates to roughly 0.22° of angular deviation — not 0.04°. The discrepancy depends on how the refraction angle is computed from n(r). Either way, both values are orders of magnitude short of the claimed +4.5°, so the conclusion holds. But precision matters in a scientific review.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the ~0.04° figure or replace with a range. The key point is that n(r) at Oslo is ~1.004, far too small to produce 4.5° of deviation. Consider stating the refractive index value directly rather than the angular deviation, to avoid ambiguity in the ray-tracing calculation.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's coordinate system uses Polaris elevation as an INPUT (r × tan(polaris) = H(r), solve for r). The +0.27° at Chapel Hill is not a prediction but an input data point. This is circular: the dome uses Polaris elevation to define its coordinate system, then claims the coordinate system 'predicts' Polaris elevation. This circularity argument is more fundamental than the error budget argument.
sources: raw-text/04-coordinates.txt: 'r(city) = solve numerically: r x tan(polaris_elevation) = H(r)'

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's coordinate table is effectively a WGS84 latitude lookup table. For 23/24 cities, Polaris elevation ≈ WGS84 latitude (±0.05°). This means the dome's coordinate system is parasitic on the globe model — it works because it imports globe-derived latitudes as inputs, not because dome geometry independently predicts them.

kernel_of_truth.description: There is a genuine (and well-documented) phenomenon where Polaris altitude does not exactly equal observer latitude. Polaris has a declination of ~89.26°, making it 0.74° from the true celestial pole. This produces a real, measurable diurnal variation. The dome model is correct that a naive measurement of Polaris altitude will not exactly equal latitude — but this is known, documented, and corrected for in all precision work using standard pole-star correction tables.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The dome does not discover this effect — it has been documented since before Tycho Brahe. The dome model does not predict the magnitude of the offset (0.74°) or its diurnal variation pattern. It merely notes a discrepancy (+0.27°) that falls entirely within the known offset range and measurement uncertainty. Renaming a known, explained effect as 'dome confirmation' adds no new knowledge.

deeper_analysis: Cycle 2 re-review confirms Cycle 1 findings and adds two significant new observations: (1) The duplicate paragraph issue — the PRED-NEW-001 retrodiction argument appears twice verbatim, which is an editing artifact that should be cleaned up. (2) The quantitative analysis of the dome's entire coordinate table reveals that Chapel Hill is the ONLY city with a notable Polaris-latitude discrepancy (+0.27°). All other 23 cities match to ±0.05°. This transforms the argument from 'the error budget explains +0.27°' to 'the dome's own data shows Polaris = latitude for 23/24 cities, confirming the globe model, with one anomalous point that falls within measurement noise.' Additionally, PRED-NEW-001's prediction of +4.5° for Oslo is directly contradicted by the dome's own coordinate table (Oslo: 59.90° vs WGS84 59.91°, i.e., 0° excess). The Cycle 1 major issues about claim mismatch between wins.json and PRED-NEW-001 appear partially addressed in the current text (PRED-NEW-001 is now mentioned), but the duplicate paragraph and missing coordinate-table argument represent clear improvement opportunities. The verdict 'Refuted by Data' remains correct and well-supported.
